g.data-narration: Welcome to Interconnection Networks! Today we explore how processors communicate in parallel computing systems.
Interconnection networks are communication backbones connecting processors, memory, and I/O devices in parallel systems.
The network choice directly impacts latency, bandwidth, scalability, and overall system performance in parallel applications.
Four critical metrics evaluate networks: latency (message travel time), bandwidth (data rate), bisection bandwidth, and diameter.
Networks classify as static (fixed connections) vs dynamic (reconfigurable), and direct (nodes part of network) vs indirect.
Bus networks use a single shared communication pathway. Simple and inexpensive but limited bandwidth and poor scalability.
Ring networks connect nodes in a circular pattern with predictable latency but vulnerability to single link failures.
Mesh networks form a grid structure with each node connecting to neighbors. Good scalability but higher latency for distant nodes.
Hypercube networks form n-dimensional cubes with 2^n nodes. They offer low diameter (log₂N) and high connectivity but complex wiring.
Crossbar switches connect any input to any output non-blocking but cost O(N²), making them expensive for large systems.
MINs use multiple switch stages for scalability. Omega network offers good performance with lower cost than crossbar.
Routing determines message paths: deterministic uses fixed routes, adaptive uses dynamic routing based on congestion for better performance.
